---
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
---

```{r}
getwd()

### Using Titanic data to show missing values and remove them ###

#Importing dataset
tit_df <- read.csv("titanic-Dataset.csv")

#Print the Structure of the dataset
str(tit_df)

#List the variables of the dataset
names(tit_df)

#Print the top 15 rows of the dataset
head(tit_df, n=15)

#Remove missing values in the dataset
sum(is.na(tit_df))

tit_df <- na.omit(tit_df)
sum(is.na(tit_df))

#Identify and remove duplicate data in the dataset
sum(duplicated(tit_df))

head(tit_df[!duplicated(tit_df),],n=30)



### Using Mall data for all other questions ###

#Importing data
mall_df <- read.csv("Mall_Customers.csv")

#Print the Structure of the dataset
str(mall_df)

#List the variables of the dataset
names(mall_df)

#Print the top 15 rows of the dataset
head(mall_df, n=15)

#Write a user defined function using any of the variables from the data set.
#Find monthly income of customer
get_monthly_income = function(annual_k) {
monthly <- annual_k / 12
return(monthly)
}
mall_df$Monthly_Income_k <- get_monthly_income(mall_df$Annual.Income..k..)
head(mall_df[, c("Annual.Income..k..", "Monthly_Income_k")], n=25)

#Use data manipulation techniques and filter rows based on any
#logical criteria that exist in your dataset.
#Filter for young high-spenders and sort them by income, Age < 30
#and Spending Score > 70
young_high_spenders <- mall_df[mall_df$Age < 30 & mall_df$Spending.Score..1.100. > 70, ]
head(young_high_spenders, n=25)

#Reorder multiple rows in descending order
mall_sorted <- mall_df[order(-mall_df$Annual.Income..k..), ]
head(mall_sorted, n = 25)

#Rename some of the column names in your dataset
names(mall_df)[4] <- "Annual_Income"
names(mall_df)[5] <- "Spending_Score"
mall_sorted <- mall_df[order(-mall_df$Annual_Income), ]
head(mall_sorted, 10)

#Add new variables in your data frame by using a mathematical function
#Quarterly income
mall_df$Quarterly_Income <- mall_df$Monthly_Income_k*4
head(mall_df,5)

#Create a training set using random number generator engine
#Initialize seed
set.seed(123)

#Determine sample size (75% for training)
data_size <- nrow(mall_df)
train_size <- floor(0.75 * data_size)

#Generate random row indices for training
#sample() picks 'train_size' unique integers from 1 to 'data_size'
train_indices <- sample(seq_len(data_size), size = train_size)

#Split the data
train_set <- mall_df[train_indices, ]  #Select the random rows

#Print the summary statistics of your dataset
summary(mall_df)

#Use any of the numerical variables from the dataset and perform the following statistical  functions
mean(mall_df$Annual_Income) #Mean
median(mall_df$Annual_Income) #Median

#Mode Function
#install.packages("modeest")
library(modeest)
mfv(mall_df$Annual_Income) #Mode

#User defined Mode function
malldf_mode <- function(x) {
  uniq_x <- unique(x)
  uniq_x[which.max(tabulate(match(x, uniq_x)))]
}
malldf_mode(mall_df$Annual_Income)


range(mall_df$Annual_Income) #Range

#Plot a scatter plot for any 2 variables in your dataset
#install.packages("ggplot2")
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mall_df, aes(x = Age, y = Annual_Income)) + 
  geom_point()

#Plot a bar plot for any 2 variables in your dataset 
ggplot(mall_df, aes(x = Gender, y = Spending_Score)) +  geom_bar(stat="identity")

#Find the correlation between any 2 variables by applying Pearson correlation
corr_p <- cor(mall_df$Spending_Score, mall_df$Annual_Income, method = "pearson")
print(corr_p)


```
